sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module One — variables and algebraic expressions. This is the starter pack. Once today clicks, the rest of algebra opens up.

Pull out your phone for a second. Your passcode — six digits, only you know them. To everyone else those digits are unknown. We could write them as x x x x x x. Each x is a placeholder for a number nobody else knows. That's a variable. Algebra is just the language we use to talk about unknown or changing numbers — and you've already been doing it without calling it that.

Three things today. One — what a variable actually is. Two — what an algebraic expression is, and the vocabulary that comes with it. Three — how to evaluate an expression when you do know the value. Easy module. Don't sleep on it though, because every other module assumes you nailed this.

A variable is a letter that stands in for a number. Either a number you don't know yet, or a number that can change. Most common letters are x, y, and z, but it can really be anything — t for time, h for height, c for cost. The letter itself doesn't matter. It's just a name we give the unknown so we can talk about it.

An algebraic expression is a combination of variables, numbers, and operations — with no equals sign. Three x plus five is an expression. So is x squared minus seven, or four y over three. An expression describes a quantity, but it doesn't claim the quantity equals anything specific. That's the whole difference between expression and equation, and it'll matter later.

Quick vocab dump. In the expression three x plus five, you've got two terms — three x and five. The number three in three x is called the coefficient — the number multiplied by the variable. The five, sitting alone with no variable, is called a constant. Coefficient, term, constant. Three words. You're going to hear them everywhere from here on out.

To evaluate an expression means plugging in a number for the variable and computing. Example one. Evaluate three x plus five when x equals two. Replace the x with a two — three times two, plus five. That's six plus five. Eleven. Done. Notice we're not solving anything. We just substituted and simplified.

Example two. Evaluate four times the quantity y minus three, when y equals seven. Inside the parentheses first — seven minus three is four. Then four times four. Sixteen. PEMDAS still applies inside expressions — parentheses go first, then multiplication. We'll review that whole order in Module Two.
Your turn. Evaluate two a plus six when a equals five. Pause the video, plug it in, come back.

Welcome back. Two times five is ten. Ten plus six is sixteen. So a plus six, with a equals five, gives sixteen. If you got it — nice. If you forgot to multiply before adding, that's PEMDAS catching you. We fix that next module.

Where this shows up in real life. Movie tickets cost twelve dollars each. Buy n tickets, total cost is twelve n. That's an algebraic expression. Buy three tickets — plug in n equals three. Twelve times three. Thirty-six dollars. The expression let us write the rule once, for any number of tickets. That's the actual point of algebra.

Recap. A variable is a letter standing for an unknown or changing number. An expression combines variables, numbers, and operations — no equals sign. The coefficient is the number multiplied by the variable, and a constant has no variable at all. To evaluate, you plug in and simplify. That's the whole module.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master this module, here's the full path. One — read OpenStax Elementary Algebra Chapter one point one. Two — go to Khan Academy and work through the twenty-problem practice set on writing expressions. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: write ten real-life situations as expressions and evaluate each. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if anything feels fuzzy. The video is maybe ten percent of learning this. The other ninety percent is reps. See you in Module Two — order of operations, PEMDAS, all that good stuff.
